narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting their likelihood of failing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.20 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 40 (above the usual 36.70 average), Year 1 school decile ranking of 2 (well below the average 5.71), and Year 3 decile of 1 (compared to the average 5.53)—and calculated a 47.8% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This elevated risk threshold led directly to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to pass on their first attempt. Among all the factors considered, your LSAT score had the strongest positive influence, pushing the prediction toward success by demonstrating stronger-than-average preparation for legal reasoning. However, your Year 3 decile ranking exerted an even more powerful counterforce, significantly increasing the failure risk because it reflects weaker performance relative to peers at that stage. Your Year 1 decile ranking also contributed toward the failure prediction, though with less intensity, highlighting early academic struggles in your school context. Compared to typical students, where full-time enrollment is the norm at 92.7%, your planned full-time status didn't sway the outcome much, but your lower deciles stood out as red flags against the averages. These top three elements—LSAT favoring you, but Year 3 and Year 1 deciles overriding it—were the key drivers tipping the scales to rejection. While your solid LSAT is a bright spot many applicants lack, the model saw your decile rankings as stronger signals of potential bar struggles, based on patterns in past students. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, as even small improvements there could shift future predictions dramatically. You might also seek targeted LSAT-level prep to maintain that edge while addressing GPA weaknesses through post-baccalaureate options or explanations in reapplications. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by focusing on these academic boosters before reapplying—your upper-middle background gives you resources to invest in that growth. With persistence, you can reshape how models like this view your potential.
